question: A couple with a 5-year-old son diagnosed with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency is expecting another child. Considering the genetic nature of G6PD deficiency, which of the following statements is true regarding the risk to the female fetus?
options: The baby has a 25% chance of clinical disease
The baby has a 50% chance of being a carrier
The baby will be a carrier
The baby has a 50% chance of clinical disease

explanation.short: B. The baby has a 50% chance of being a carrier
explanation.detail:
Ans.  B. The baby has a 50% chance of being a carrier

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • G6PD deficiency is an X-linked recessive disorder. Here's how the inheritance works:
  • • Males (XY): If they inherit the defective X chromosome, they express the disease because they have only one X. Females (XX): Need two defective X chromosomes to express the disease. If they inherit one defective X, they are typically carriers.
  • • Males (XY): If they inherit the defective X chromosome, they express the disease because they have only one X.
  • • Males (XY):
  • • Females (XX): Need two defective X chromosomes to express the disease. If they inherit one defective X, they are typically carriers.
  • • Females (XX):
  • • In this scenario:
  • • In this scenario:
  • • The son is affected, meaning he must have inherited the defective X chromosome from his mother. Both parents are clinically unaffected, so: The mother is likely a carrier (XᴺXᴰ), and The father is normal (XᴺY).
  • • The son is affected, meaning he must have inherited the defective X chromosome from his mother.
  • • Both parents are clinically unaffected, so: The mother is likely a carrier (XᴺXᴰ), and The father is normal (XᴺY).
  • • The mother is likely a carrier (XᴺXᴰ), and The father is normal (XᴺY).
  • • The mother is likely a carrier (XᴺXᴰ), and
  • • The father is normal (XᴺY).
  • • When a carrier mother (XᴺXᴰ) and a normal father (XᴺY) have a daughter, the daughter has:
  • • 50% chance of being a carrier (XᴺXᴰ) 50% chance of being completely normal (XᴺXᴺ)
  • • 50% chance of being a carrier (XᴺXᴰ)
  • • 50% chance of being completely normal (XᴺXᴺ)
  • • Thus, the risk to the female fetus is 50% chance of being a carrier.
  • • Other Options:
  • • Other Options:
  • • Option A. 25% chance of clinical disease : Incorrect. A female would need two defective X chromosomes to be affected, which isn't the case here. Option C. The baby will be a carrier : Incorrect. There’s a 50% chance, not 100%. Option D. 50% chance of clinical disease : Incorrect. Females are typically only carriers unless both X chromosomes are defective.
  • • Option A. 25% chance of clinical disease : Incorrect. A female would need two defective X chromosomes to be affected, which isn't the case here.
  • • Option A. 25% chance of clinical disease
  • • Option C. The baby will be a carrier : Incorrect. There’s a 50% chance, not 100%.
  • • Option C. The baby will be a carrier
  • • Option D. 50% chance of clinical disease : Incorrect. Females are typically only carriers unless both X chromosomes are defective.
  • • Option D. 50% chance of clinical disease

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ G6PD deficiency is the most common enzyme deficiency leading to hemolytic anemia. It affects the pentose phosphate pathway, reducing the production of NADPH, which is essential for maintaining reduced glutathione that protects cells from oxidative damage. This condition predominantly affects males, while female carriers are usually asymptomatic.

question: A 32-year-old woman who has inherited one mutant allele of the BRCA1 gene has a 60% risk of developing breast cancer by age 50. The BRCA1 gene encodes a protein that plays a crucial role in the repair of DNA double-strand breaks. Which DNA repair process is most likely impaired by a deficiency in BRCA1 protein function?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D. Homologous recombination

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A. Removal of thymine dimers: This is generally managed by nucleotide excision repair , not by homologous recombination.
  • • Option
  • • A. Removal of thymine dimers:
  • • managed by nucleotide excision repair
  • • Option B. Removal of RNA primers: RNA primers are removed during DNA replication , a process unrelated to the repair function of BRCA1.
  • • Option
  • • B. Removal of RNA primers:
  • • removed during DNA replication
  • • Option C. Removal of carcinogen adducts: Like thymine dimers, carcinogen adducts are typically addressed by nucleotide excision repair .
  • • Option
  • • C. Removal of carcinogen adducts:
  • • addressed by nucleotide excision repair

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ BRCA1 protein is fundamentally involved in the repair of DNA double-strand breaks primarily through homologous recombination , a critical process during cell division that ensures the accurate repair of DNA to prevent mutations that could lead to cancer . When BRCA1 function is compromised, the efficiency of homologous recombination repair is significantly reduced, which increases the risk of genomic instability and contributes to cancer predisposition, particularly breast and ovarian cancers.

question: Which of the following statements about the bonds between A-T base pairs is true?
options: A-T base pairs are connected by three hydrogen bonds.
A-T base pairs are connected by two hydrogen bonds.

explanation.short: B. A-T base pairs are connected by two hydrogen bonds.
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) A-T base pairs are connected by two hydrogen bonds.

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A: A-T base pairs are connected by three hydrogen bonds - This is incorrect because A-T base pairs form only two hydrogen bonds . It's the G-C base pairs that are connected by three hydrogen bonds.
  • • Option A: A-T base pairs are connected by three hydrogen bonds -
  • • A-T base pairs form
  • • two hydrogen bonds
  • • Option C: A-T base pairs are connected by triple covalent bonds - This is incorrect as A-T base pairs are connected by hydrogen bonds , not covalent bonds. Hydrogen bonds are weaker and more suitable for the dynamic processes of DNA replication and transcription.
  • • Option C: A-T base pairs are connected by triple covalent bonds -
  • • A-T base pairs
  • • connected by hydrogen bonds
  • • Option D: A-T base pairs do not form hydrogen bonds - This is incorrect as A-T base pairs do form hydrogen bonds ; specifically, they form two hydrogen bonds.
  • • Option D: A-T base pairs do not form hydrogen bonds -
  • • A-T base pairs do form hydrogen bonds

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Hydrogen Bonding: The DNA strands are held together by hydrogen bonds between the purine and pyrimidine bases. There are three hydrogen bonds formed between C and G and Two Hydrogen bonds formed between A and T . Hence, the GC bond is stronger than the AT bond.
  • ➤ Hydrogen Bonding: The DNA strands are held together by hydrogen bonds between the purine and pyrimidine bases.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Which of the following cell types do not have the enzyme telomerase that protects the length of telomeres at the chromosomal ends?

explanation.detail:
Ans.

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Telomerase is an enzyme that adds repetitive nucleotide sequences to the ends of chromosomes (telomeres), thereby preventing their progressive shortening during DNA replication. This is especially important in cells that divide frequently or require long-term proliferative potential.
  • • Telomerase
  • • Somatic cells , which constitute the majority of cells in the body (like skin, muscle, and liver cells), lack significant telomerase activity . As a result, with each cell division, telomeres become progressively shorter. Eventually, this leads to cellular senescence or programmed cell death (apoptosis), which is a natural part of aging.
  • • Somatic cells
  • • lack significant telomerase activity
  • • In contrast:
  • • Germinal (germ) cells have active telomerase to ensure genomic stability is passed on to offspring. Tumor cells often reactivate telomerase , which enables them to escape normal cellular aging and divide indefinitely — a key feature of cancer. Hemopoietic stem cells (in bone marrow) also express telomerase to maintain their replicative capacity and continually replenish blood cells.
  • • Germinal (germ) cells have active telomerase to ensure genomic stability is passed on to offspring.
  • • Germinal (germ) cells
  • • Tumor cells often reactivate telomerase , which enables them to escape normal cellular aging and divide indefinitely — a key feature of cancer.
  • • Tumor cells
  • • reactivate telomerase
  • • Hemopoietic stem cells (in bone marrow) also express telomerase to maintain their replicative capacity and continually replenish blood cells.
  • • Hemopoietic stem cells

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Understand that somatic cells typically lack telomerase , which causes telomere shortening over time and contributes to the natural aging process and cellular lifespan limitation. This contrasts with cell types like germinal, stem, and cancer cells, where telomerase activity supports sustained proliferation.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Which statement accurately reflects Chargaff's rule regarding the base composition of DNA?"

explanation.detail:
Ans.

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Chargaff’s rule describes the base composition in double-stranded DNA. It states that in any double-stranded DNA molecule:
  • • The amount of adenine (A) equals thymine (T) The amount of guanine (G) equals cytosine (C)
  • • The amount of adenine (A) equals thymine (T)
  • • adenine (A)
  • • thymine (T)
  • • The amount of guanine (G) equals cytosine (C)
  • • guanine (G)
  • • cytosine (C)
  • • Since adenine and guanine are purines , and thymine and cytosine are pyrimidines , the total number of purines (A + G) is equal to the total number of pyrimidines (T + C) .
  • • purines
  • • pyrimidines
  • • total number of purines (A + G)
  • • total number of pyrimidines (T + C)
  • • This is why A + G = T + C is the correct expression of Chargaff’s rule.
  • • A + G = T + C
  • • Other Options:
  • • Other Options:
  • • Option B: A/T = G/C — Incorrect. Chargaff’s rule is about absolute quantities being equal (A = T, G = C), not about the ratios between them.
  • • Option B: A/T = G/C
  • • ratios
  • • Option C: A = U = T = G = C — Incorrect. Uracil (U) is not found in DNA; it’s found in RNA. Also, the quantities of all bases are not equal in DNA.
  • • Option C: A = U = T = G = C
  • • Option D: A + T = G + C — Incorrect. There is no rule or biochemical principle stating that the sum of A and T equals G and C. The actual base pairing rules state A = T and G = C individually, which leads to A + G = T + C.
  • • Option D: A + T = G + C

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Understand that Chargaff’s rule applies to double-stranded DNA, reflecting base pairing: A = T and G = C. Thus, the number of purines (A + G) equals the number of pyrimidines (T + C) . This principle laid the foundation for the discovery of the DNA double helix structure.
  • ➤ Chargaff’s rule
  • ➤ purines (A + G)
  • ➤ pyrimidines (T + C)
  • ➤ Ref : Harper 30/e page 360.
  • ➤ Ref

subject: Biochemistry
question: Which of the following aspects of DNA structure is most significantly responsible for its thermo-stability?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B. G – C Base Pairs

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • The thermal stability of DNA is primarily determined by its base composition , particularly the number of guanine-cytosine (G≡C) base pairs . Each G≡C pair is held together by three hydrogen bonds , whereas each A=T pair has only two hydrogen bonds . The additional hydrogen bond in G≡C base pairs increases the energy required to separate the DNA strands during denaturation, leading to a higher melting temperature (Tm) .
  • • base composition
  • • number of guanine-cytosine (G≡C) base pairs
  • • three hydrogen bonds
  • • two hydrogen bonds
  • • higher melting temperature (Tm)
  • • Regions of DNA that are G-C rich therefore exhibit greater thermal stability and resist denaturation at higher temperatures compared to A-T rich regions.
  • • G-C rich
  • • Other Options:
  • • Other Options:
  • • Option A: A = T – A-T base pairs are part of DNA structure but are less thermally stable due to only two hydrogen bonds.
  • • Option A: A = T
  • • Option C: Molecular base – This is vague and not a scientifically specific factor; it does not explain thermal stability.
  • • Option C: Molecular base
  • • Option D: Parallel arrangement – This is incorrect because DNA strands are actually antiparallel , not parallel. Also, the antiparallel nature contributes more to structural alignment and enzymatic interactions than to thermal stability.
  • • Option D: Parallel arrangement
  • • antiparallel

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Understand that G≡C base pairing contributes more significantly to DNA thermal stability than A=T pairing, due to the presence of an extra hydrogen bond . This makes G-C rich DNA sequences harder to denature, increasing the melting temperature (Tm) of the molecule.
  • ➤ G≡C base pairing
  • ➤ extra hydrogen bond
  • ➤ G-C rich DNA sequences
  • ➤ melting temperature (Tm)
  • ➤ Ref: Harper 30/e: p 304-306

question: Among the listed eukaryotic promoter/regulatory elements, which one exhibits the greatest variability in position relative to the transcription start site?

Explanation:

  • • Option A. Downstream promoter element (DPE): The DPE is located relatively close to the core promoter , usually within about 30 base pairs downstream of the transcription start site, and its position is not as variable as that of enhancers.
  • • Option A. Downstream promoter element (DPE):
  • • located relatively close
  • • core promoter
  • • within about 30 base pairs downstream

  • • core promoter element
  • • transcription start site
  • • Option D. Operator: Operators are DNA sequences that interact with repressors to control the transcription of adjacent genes , mainly in prokaryotes . In eukaryotes, similar regulatory elements are part of the promoter or enhancers but are not typically referred to as operators.

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Eukaryotic promoters are much more complex than bacterial promoters . The most common bacterial promoter elements are the –10 and –35 sequences , which are the recognition sites for RNA polymerase binding. In addition, the bacterial promoters may have operators (repressor binding sites) and activator binding sites . Eukaryotic promoters have a core region that may contain a TATA box (which is bound by TBP, the TATA binding protein), an initiator sequence , and downstream promoter elements . In addition, eukaryotic promoters have upstream elements such as GC boxes and CCAAT boxes which bind specific transcription factors (Sp1 and CTF, respectively). Enhancers are elements that increase or enhance the rate of transcription initiation from a promoter . Enhancers can be upstream or downstream of the transcription start site and can exert their effect from hundreds or thousands of bases away and also they are orientation-independent (ie, whether their recognition sequence is on the Watson or Crick strand).

question: A 55-year-old obese woman visits her primary care physician complaining of recurrent episodes of mild epigastric pain and vomiting, particularly after consuming fatty meals. FUNCTION : The gallbladder stores bile salts produced by the liver and when it is contracted by the entry of food → bile salts travel through the bile duct to the duodenum → causes emulsification of fats , thereby helping in digestion of fat. When the solutes in the gallbladder precipitate , they form gallstones. Patients may have pain from the gallstones, usually after a fatty meal . subject: Biochemistry
question: Assertion (A): Metachromatic leukodystrophy is a glycogen storage disease characterized by mental retardation, organomegaly, etc. Reasoning (R): This is due to deficiency of enzyme hexosaminidase A. Select the correct option:
options: A and R both are right but R is not the Correct explanation of A
A and R both are wrong
A is wrong but R is a right statement
A as well as R are right and R is a correct explanation of A

explanation.short: B. A and R both are wrong
explanation.detail:
Ans. B. A and R both are wrong

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Assertion (A) is wrong because Metachromatic Leukodystrophy (MLD) is not a glycogen storage disease . It is a lysosomal storage disorder that belongs to the group of sphingolipidoses , caused by a deficiency in the enzyme arylsulfatase A . This leads to the accumulation of sulfatides , particularly sulfogalactocerebroside , resulting in demyelination of white matter , particularly in the central and peripheral nervous systems.

  • • neurological deterioration

subject: Biochemistry
question: What is the relationship between this finding and the function of LCAT? Assertion (A): Excess cholesterol in the cell is esterified into cholesterol ester and is stored up there. Reasoning (R): LCAT converts free cholesterol to the cholesterol ester and plays a major role in reverse cholesterol transport.
options: A and R both are wrong
A as well as R are right and R is a correct explanation of A
A is wrong but R is a right statement
A and R both are right but R is not the correct explanation of A

explanation.short: D. A and R both are right but R is not the correct explanation of A
explanation.detail:
Ans. D. A and R both are right but R is not the correct explanation of A

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Assertion (A) is correct — excess intracellular cholesterol is esterified and stored in the cholesterol pool within cells. This esterification allows for non-toxic storage of cholesterol in lipid droplets. However, the enzyme responsible for this process is ACAT (Acyl-CoA:cholesterol acyltransferase) , which is intracellular , not LCAT. Reason (R) is also correct — LCAT (Lecithin:cholesterol acyltransferase) is a plasma enzyme bound to HDL , and it converts free cholesterol (acquired from peripheral tissues) into cholesterol esters as part of reverse cholesterol transport , allowing HDL to carry cholesterol back to the liver.
  • • Assertion (A) is correct — excess intracellular cholesterol is esterified and stored in the cholesterol pool within cells. This esterification allows for non-toxic storage of cholesterol in lipid droplets.

  • • correct

subject: Biochemistry
question: Match the following enzymes with the pathway they are involved in:

explanation.detail:
Ans. A) 1-b, 2-a, 3-d, 4-c

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • HMG CoA Reductase is a key enzyme in the biosynthesis of cholesterol , catalyzing the conversion of HMG-CoA to mevalonate , a rate-limiting step in cholesterol synthesis. Hence, 1-b is correct . 7-alpha-hydroxylase is the rate-limiting enzyme in the formation of bile acids from cholesterol . It catalyzes the introduction of a hydroxyl group at the 7th position of the steroid nucleus of cholesterol, which is the first step in the classical pathway of bile acid synthesis . Thus, 2-a is correct. Thiophorase , also known as succinyl-CoA:3-ketoacid coenzyme A transferase (SCOT), is involved in ketolysis, particularly in the utilization of ketone bodies by the tissues outside the liver, converting acetoacetate into acetoacetyl-CoA. Therefore, 3-d is accurate. Carnitine acyl transferase I is a crucial enzyme in the beta-oxidation of fatty acids . It facilitates the transport of fatty acids into the mitochondria for oxidation by converting them into their acyl-carnitine derivatives , which can cross the mitochondrial membrane . Hence, 4-c is correct.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Assertion (A): Bile acid protects the body from cholesterol over production. Reasoning (R): In liver, choles terol is continuously converted to bile acid.
options: A and R both are right, but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is wrong but R is a right statement
A as well as R are right, and R is a correct explanation of A
A and R both are wrong

explanation.short: C. A as well as R are right, and R is a correct explanation of A
explanation.detail:
Ans. C)

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Assertion (A) is correct. Bile acids , which are derived from cholesterol, play a significant role in maintaining cholesterol homeostasis within the body. By converting cholesterol into bile acids , the body can regulate the levels of cholesterol, preventing its overproduction and accumulation , which can lead to conditions such as hypercholesterolemia and atherosclerosis.
  • • Assertion (A)
  • • correct. Bile acids
  • • derived
  • • cholesterol,
  • • role
  • • cholesterol homeostasis
  • • body.
  • • cholesterol into bile acids
  • • levels of cholesterol, preventing
  • • overproduction and accumulation
  • • hypercholesterolemia and atherosclerosis.
  • • Reasoning (R) is also correct and provides a correct explanation for Assertion (A) . The liver plays a crucial role in lipid metabolism , including the conversion of cholesterol into bile acids . This conversion is an important regulatory mechanism for controlling cholesterol levels in the body. Bile acids are then excreted into the intestine with bile , where they aid in the digestion and absorption of fats and fat-soluble vitamins. Some bile acids are reabsorbed from the intestine and returned to the liver, while others are lost in the feces . This loss of bile acids from the body necessitates the conversion of more cholesterol into bile acids, thus helping to regulate cholesterol levels and prevent overproduction.

It occurs due to vomiting against a closed glottis , which leads to a sudden increase in esophageal pressure and typically results in perforation of the lower esophagus.

  • • cause esophageal perforation

  • • Option D: Penetrating trauma to chest. Penetrating trauma is another possible cause of esophageal injury , but again, it is not as common as iatrogenic causes.

  • • another possible cause of esophageal injury

Esophageal perforation can occur due to instrumental procedures such as upper GI endoscopy , dilatation, and stenting. Spontaneous esophageal perforation , also known as Boerhaave syndrome , can occur when vomiting against a closed glottis , resulting in a massive increase in esophageal pressure and typically perforating the lower esophagus over the left pleura. CT with water-soluble contrast is the ideal imaging modality to diagnose esophageal perforation. Treatment options for esophageal perforation include conservative management for small perforations and surgery for septic load (Boerhaave syndrome), unstable mediastinitis, or intra-abdominal esophageal perforation.

Caustic injury does not cause tissue damage

  • • premalignant condition

question: A 5-year-old child accidentally swallowed a foreign object while playing. What is the most dangerous foreign object that a child can swallow?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Battery

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A: Food bolus . A food bolus can cause obstruction but is typically not as dangerous as other objects because it often softens and passes through the gastrointestinal tract or can be removed endoscopically without causing significant damage.
  • • Option A: Food bolus
  • • obstruction
  • • not as dangerous as other objects because it often softens and passes through the gastrointestinal tract
  • • Option B: Fish Bone . A fish bone can be sharp and potentially puncture the esophagus or other parts of the gastrointestinal tract . However, it is generally less harmful compared to corrosive or electrically charged objects because it does not cause tissue necrosis or systemic toxicity.
  • • Option B: Fish Bone
  • • sharp and potentially puncture the esophagus
  • • gastrointestinal tract
  • • less harmful compared to corrosive or electrically charged objects
  • • does not cause tissue necrosis
  • • Option D: Coin. A coin can become lodged in the esophagus , particularly at one of the natural constrictions , but it generally does not cause the same degree of tissue damage as a battery . The coin can often be retrieved endoscopically without significant complication.
  • • Option D: Coin.
  • • lodged in the esophagus
  • • one of the natural constrictions
  • • does not cause the same degree of tissue damage as a battery

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The most dangerous foreign object that a child can swallow is a battery due to the risk of electrical discharge , leakage of caustic substances, and the potential for rapid tissue damage and necrosis.
  • ➤ most dangerous foreign object
  • ➤ child can swallow is a battery
  • ➤ risk of electrical discharge
  • ➤ Swallowing a foreign object can be a serious medical emergency, especially in children. The most common foreign objects that are swallowed are food boluses and bones . However, the most dangerous foreign object that a child can swallow is a battery. The esophagus has several constrictions where foreign objects can become lodged. The three most common locations for foreign body impaction are at the cricopharynx (15cm from the incisors), at the left bronchus crossing the esophagus (25cm from the incisors), and at the diaphragmatic pleura (40cm from the incisors). The first step in managing a swallowed foreign body is to obtain a chest X-ray to determine the location of the object. If the object is in the esophagus or stomach, endoscopic retrieval may be possible . However, if the object is a battery, it must be removed as an emergency via endoscopy or colonoscopy , depending on the location of the foreign body.
  • ➤ Swallowing a foreign object can be a serious medical emergency, especially in children. The most common foreign objects that are swallowed are food boluses and bones . However, the most dangerous foreign object that a child can swallow is a battery.

subject: Biochemistry
question: A 30-year-old female presents with colicky abdominal pain and bilious vomiting. She reports weight loss following a keto-diet in the past few months. On investigation, the small bowel appears normal, but a CECT of the abdomen reveals compression of the D3 segment of the duodenum due to narrowing of the angle of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA). Which of the following is the best option for treatment of this condition?
options: Administration of medications to relieve pain
Feeding the patient to promote weight gain
Endoscopic intervention to widen the narrowed angle

explanation.short: C. Feeding the patient to promote weight gain
explanation.detail:
Ans. C) Feeding the patient to promote weight gain

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A: Surgery to remove the obstruction . Surgical intervention may be considered if conservative measures fail . It is not the first line of treatment because the syndrome is often caused by a lack of mesenteric fat padding, not an actual physical obstruction that needs to be removed.
  • • Option A: Surgery to remove the obstruction
  • • Surgical intervention
  • • conservative measures fail
  • • not the first line of treatment
  • • Option B: Administration of medications to relieve pain . While pain management is an important aspect of patient care , medications alone will not address the underlying issue of the narrowed angle between the SMA and the aorta, which is causing the duodenal compression.
  • • Option B: Administration of medications to relieve pain
  • • pain management is an important aspect of patient care
  • • medications alone will not address the underlying issue
  • • Option D: Endoscopic intervention to widen the narrowed angle . Endoscopic intervention is not the standard treatment for SMA Syndrome because the problem is not within the duodenal lumen but rather external compression.
  • • Option D: Endoscopic intervention to widen the narrowed angle
  • • Endoscopic intervention
  • • not the standard treatment for SMA Syndrome

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The initial treatment for SMA Syndrome is conservative , focusing on nutritional support to promote weight gain and increase the mesenteric fat pad, thereby relieving the compression of the duodenum.
  • ➤ initial treatment for SMA Syndrome is conservative
  • ➤ SMA Syndrome , also known as Wilkie Syndrome , is a rare cause of intestinal obstruction . It occurs due to the narrowing of the angle between the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and aorta, leading to compression of the third part of the duodenum (D3) .
  • ➤ SMA Syndrome , also known as Wilkie Syndrome , is a rare cause of intestinal obstruction . It occurs due to the narrowing of the angle between the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and aorta, leading to compression of the third part of the duodenum (D3) .
  • ➤ SMA Syndrome
  • ➤ Wilkie Syndrome
  • ➤ rare cause of intestinal obstruction
  • ➤ compression of the third part of the duodenum (D3)
  • ➤ Risk factors:
  • ➤ Risk factors:
  • ➤ Sudden weight loss Anorexia nervosa Prolonged bed rest Scoliosis The clinical features of SMA Syndrome include colicky abdominal pain, bilious vomiting, and weight loss. On investigation, the small bowel appears normal, but a CECT of the abdomen may reveal compression of the D3 segment of the duodenum due to narrowing of the angle of the SMA. The initial treatment for SMA Syndrome is conservative and involves feeding the patient to promote weight gain and open the narrowed angle. This can be achieved through the placement of a naso-jejunostomy tube. If this fails, surgery may be required to bypass the duodenum and relieve the obstruction.
  • ➤ Sudden weight loss
  • ➤ Anorexia nervosa
  • ➤ Prolonged bed rest
  • ➤ Scoliosis

  • ➤ CECT of the abdomen may reveal compression of the D3 segment of the duodenum
  • ➤ The initial treatment for SMA Syndrome is conservative and involves feeding the patient to promote weight gain and open the narrowed angle. This can be achieved through the placement of a naso-jejunostomy tube. If this fails, surgery may be required to bypass the duodenum and relieve the obstruction.

subject: Biochemistry
question: A 50-year-old male patient with a history of peptic ulcer disease presents with a complaint of non-bilious vomiting. On examination, a succussion splash is heard over abdomen. CECT reveals the presence of a pyloric stricture. Which of the following is the first step for management of this condition?
options: Conservative management with nasogastric decompression and washout
Surgical reconstruction using Billroth II procedure
Mikulicz stricturoplasty to relieve the obstruction

explanation.short: A. Conservative management with nasogastric decompression and washout
explanation.detail:
Ans. A) Conservative management with nasogastric decompression and washout

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option B: Surgical reconstruction using Billroth II procedure . The Billroth II procedure is a surgical option (gastro-jejunostomy) that is generally considered after conservative management has failed.
  • • Option B: Surgical reconstruction using Billroth II procedure
  • • surgical option
  • • generally considered after conservative management has failed.
  • • Option C: Endoscopic dilatation of the stricture . Endoscopic dilatation is a less invasive treatment option compared to surgery and can be effective in managing strictures . However, it is not the first-line treatment before attempting nasogastric decompression.
  • • Option C: Endoscopic dilatation of the stricture
  • • less invasive treatment option compared to surgery
  • • effective in managing strictures
  • • Option D: Mikulicz strictureplasty to relieve the obstruction . Mikulicz strictureplasty is a surgical technique used when strictures are not amenable to endoscopic dilatation . It is considered a more advanced step if other less invasive measures fail.
  • • Option D: Mikulicz strictureplasty to relieve the obstruction
  • • surgical technique used when strictures are not amenable to endoscopic dilatation

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The first step in managing a pyloric stricture is conservative management with nasogastric decompression and washout to provide symptomatic relief
  • ➤ first step in managing a pyloric stricture
  • ➤ conservative management
  • ➤ nasogastric decompression
  • ➤ Pyloric stricture is a late complication of peptic ulcer disease , which occurs due to the fibrosis and scarring that develop when the circumferential ulcers over the stomach heal. This can lead to narrowing of the stomach or gastric outlet obstruction, which usually occurs at the first part of the duodenum. Clinical features of pyloric stricture include a history of peptic ulcer disease and non-bilious vomiting . The management of pyloric stricture depends on the extent and severity of the obstruction . Initial conservative management involves nasogastric decompression and washout , which can provide symptomatic relief. Endoscopy can be used to confirm the presence of a stricture and to obtain a biopsy to rule out adenocarcinoma . If the stricture is benign, it can be treated with dilatation. If dilatation fails, surgery may be required to relieve the obstruction. Mikulicz stricturoplasty is a surgical procedure that involves opening the pylorus horizontally and closing it vertically to relieve the obstruction . This is a preferred option when the stricture is not amenable to endoscopic dilatation. Billroth II reconstruction is another surgical option that involves removal of the pylorus and reconstruction of the gastrointestinal tract to improve gastric emptying.
  • ➤ Pyloric stricture is a late complication of peptic ulcer disease , which occurs due to the fibrosis and scarring that develop when the circumferential ulcers over the stomach heal. This can lead to narrowing of the stomach or gastric outlet obstruction, which usually occurs at the first part of the duodenum.

question: A 60-year-old male, a known case of chronic atrial fibrillation presents with acute abdominal pain and PR bleed. Abdominal examination revealed vague tenderness and diminished bowel sounds. Which of the following is the investigation of choice for diagnosis?

explanation.detail:
Ans. D) CECT abdomen angiogram

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A: Serum lipase. While serum lipase can be useful in diagnosing pancreatitis , it would not be the most helpful initial test for a patient suspected of having mesenteric ischemia.
  • • Option A: Serum lipase.
  • • serum lipase can be useful in diagnosing pancreatitis
  • • Option B: USG abdomen. An ultrasound of the abdomen can be useful for assessing many abdominal conditions, but it may not be the best choice for evaluating blood flow or identifying mesenteric ischemia, especially in an acute setting.
  • • Option B: USG abdomen.
  • • ultrasound of the abdomen can be useful for assessing many abdominal conditions,
  • • Option C: X-ray upper abdomen erect. An erect abdominal X-ray can show signs of bowel obstruction or free air under the diaphragm in cases of perforation but is not the most sensitive or specific test for mesenteric ischemia.
  • • Option C: X-ray upper abdomen erect.
  • • erect abdominal X-ray
  • • show signs of bowel obstruction or free air under the diaphragm

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:

  • ➤ context of chronic atrial fibrillation
  • ➤ next best investigation is a CECT abdomen angiogram.
  • ➤ SMA emboli may be carried from the left atrium in atrial fibrillation , the left ventricle after mural myocardial infarction , vegetations on mitral and aortic valves associated with endocarditis or an atheromatous plaque from an aortic aneurysm Investigation for mesenteric ischemia includes a CECT scan, which can determine whether the occlusion is acute or chronic and assess bowel viability or gangrene. Treatment for mesenteric ischemia depends on the extent and severity of the obstruction. Conservative management with antibiotics and bowel rest may be used in some cases. For acute occlusion, mesenteric vascular intervention is the preferred treatment . If there is evidence of gangrene, laparotomy and bowel resection may be required. Chronic mesenteric ischemia typically presents with mesenteric angina (central abdominal pain) and diarrhoea. A CECT angiogram can reveal atherosclerosis of the mesentery. Treatment for chronic mesenteric ischemia involves mesenteric angioplasty bypass.
  • ➤ SMA emboli may be carried from the left atrium in atrial fibrillation , the left ventricle after mural myocardial infarction , vegetations on mitral and aortic valves associated with endocarditis or an atheromatous plaque from an aortic aneurysm

subject: Biochemistry
question: What is the management protocol for enterocutaneous fistula?
options: Sepsis control and skin care, TPN, CECT, and planned repair
Antibiotics, enteral nutrition, MRI, and surgical intervention
Conservative management, low-residue diet, ultrasound, and outpatient follow-up
Bowel rest, antibiotics, barium enema, and observation

explanation.short: A. Sepsis control and skin care, TPN, CECT, and planned repair
explanation.detail:
Ans. A) Sepsis control and skin care, TPN, CECT, and planned repair

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option B: Antibiotics, enteral nutrition, MRI, and surgical intervention . While antibiotics and surgical intervention may be part of the management , enteral nutrition may not be feasible with a high-output fistula, and MRI is not the standard imaging modality for this condition.
  • • Option B: Antibiotics, enteral nutrition, MRI, and surgical intervention
  • • antibiotics
  • • surgical intervention
  • • management
  • • enteral nutrition
  • • not be feasible
  • • Option C: Conservative management, low-residue diet, ultrasound, and outpatient follow-up . Conservative management may be indicated in some cases , but this option lacks the aggressive approach often required for enterocutaneous fistulas, such as TPN and detailed imaging.
  • • Option C: Conservative management, low-residue diet, ultrasound, and outpatient follow-up
  • • Conservative management
  • • indicated in some cases
  • • Option D: Bowel rest, antibiotics, barium enema, and observation . Bowel rest and observation are part of the initial management , but merely antibiotics might not be sufficient for sepsis control , and a barium enema is not typically used for fistula assessment due to the risk of extravasation and worsening sepsis.
  • • Option D: Bowel rest, antibiotics, barium enema, and observation
  • • Bowel rest
  • • observation
  • • part of the initial management
  • • antibiotics might not be sufficient for sepsis control

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The management protocol for enterocutaneous fistula involves sepsis control, skin care, total parenteral nutrition (TPN), anatomical delineation with a CECT scan , and planned surgical repair following the SNAP protocol.
  • ➤ management protocol for enterocutaneous fistula
  • ➤ sepsis control, skin care, total parenteral nutrition
  • ➤ anatomical delineation with a CECT scan
  • ➤ planned surgical repair
  • ➤ Enterocutaneous fistula is an abnormal communication between the bowel and the skin, which can be caused by postoperative complications, Crohn's disease, GI malignancies, or radiation enteritis . The management of enterocutaneous fistula is based on the SNAP protocol . Sepsis control and skin care are essential for the management of enterocutaneous fistula, along with total parenteral nutrition (TPN) to provide adequate nutrition. Anatomical delineation, typically through a CECT scan, is necessary to identify the extent of the fistula and plan for repair. Planned repair is the ultimate goal of management, but high-output fistulas (>500ml) may not close spontaneously , and additional interventions may be required. Conservative management, a low-residue diet, and ultrasound are not effective for managing enterocutaneous fistula, and surgical intervention is required for definitive treatment.
  • ➤ Enterocutaneous fistula is an abnormal communication between the bowel and the skin, which can be caused by postoperative complications, Crohn's disease, GI malignancies, or radiation enteritis . The management of enterocutaneous fistula is based on the SNAP protocol .

question: A young schizophrenic female presents with abdominal pain and vomiting. The surgeon notices that she has scanty hair on scalp, and on palpation of abdomen there is crepitus in the stomach. She is likely to be suffering from?

explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Trichobezoar

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A: Phytobezoar. Phytobezoars are composed of indigestible plant material and are typically found in patients with impaired gastric motility , often secondary to gastric surgery, and not necessarily associated with psychiatric conditions or hair ingestion.
  • • Option A: Phytobezoar.
  • • composed of indigestible plant material and are typically found in patients with impaired gastric motility
  • • Option C: Ca stomach. Gastric cancer (Ca stomach) could present with abdominal pain and vomiting but would not typically present with crepitus in the stomach or be associated with the ingestion of hair.
  • • Option C: Ca stomach.
  • • Gastric cancer
  • • present with abdominal pain and vomiting but would not typically present with crepitus
  • • Option D: Gastric volvulus. Gastric volvulus presents with acute abdominal pain and may lead to vomiting, but it is unlikely to be associated with hair ingestion or crepitus on abdominal palpation unless there is associated gastric strangulation and perforation, which is a medical emergency.
  • • Option D: Gastric volvulus.
  • • acute abdominal pain and may lead to vomiting, but it is unlikely to be associated with hair ingestion or crepitus on abdominal palpation
  • • The clinical picture provided most consistently aligns with a trichobezoar , given the patient's psychiatric history and the specific finding of crepitus in the stomach on palpation.
  • • trichobezoar

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ In a young psychiatric patient presenting with abdominal pain, vomiting, and evidence of scanty scalp hair, along with palpable crepitus in the stomach, the likely diagnosis is a trichobezoar.
  • ➤ Trichobezoar (hair balls) are unusual and are virtually exclusively found in young psychiatric patients. It is caused by the ingestion of hair, which remains undigested in the stomach. The hair ball can lead to ulceration and gastrointestinal bleeding, perforation or obstruction . The diagnosis is made easily at endoscopy. Treatment consists of removal of the bezoar, which may require open surgical treatment. Phytobezoars are made of vegetable matter and are found principally in patients who have gastric stasis, usually following gastric surgery.

question: A 49 years old male presents with long-standing vague abdominal symptoms, early satiety, episodic abdominal pain, associated with vomiting. His imaging shows the following picture. What is the condition he is suffering from?

explanation.detail:
Ans. C) SMA syndrome

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A: Gastric volvulus . Gastric volvulus is an abnormal rotation of the stomach, which would typically show a distended stomach with an abnormal orientation on imaging . It often presents acutely with severe abdominal pain and retching without vomitus.

  • • abnormal rotation of the stomach, which would typically show a distended stomach with an abnormal orientation on imaging
  • • Option B: Ca stomach . Cancer of the stomach may present with nonspecific symptoms like weight loss, early satiety, and possibly a mass or irregularity on imaging , but it does not typically cause the acute angulation of the SMA leading to duodenal compression.

  • • Option D: Gastric ulcer . A gastric ulcer may present with abdominal pain and symptoms related to eating , but it would not cause the compression of the duodenum as seen in SMA syndrome.

  • ➤ Superior mesenteric artery (SMA) syndrome , also known as Wilkie syndrome is a rare acquired vascular compression disorder in which acute angulation of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) results in compression of the third part of the duodenum, leading to obstruction. Patients with SMA syndrome may present acutely, with chronic insidious symptomatology, or with an acute exacerbation of chronic symptoms: Acute presentation is usually characterised by signs and symptoms of duodenal obstruction Chronic cases may present with long-standing vague abdominal symptoms, early satiety and anorexia, or recurrent episodes of abdominal pain, associated with vomiting.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Which of these is incorrect about caustic injury to upper gastro intestinal tract?
options: Zargar’s grading is used for severity of injury
Multiple esophageal ulcers warrant urgent cervical esophagostomy

explanation.short: D. Multiple esophageal ulcers warrant urgent cervical esophagostomy
explanation.detail:
Ans. D) Multiple esophageal ulcers warrant urgent cervical esophagostomy

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A: Alkali ingestion is more dangerous . Ingestion of alkali substances is indeed more dangerous compared to acids because alkalis cause liquefactive necrosis , allowing deeper tissue penetration and damage which can lead to more severe complications such as perforation.
  • • Option A: Alkali ingestion is more dangerous
  • • Ingestion of alkali substances
  • • dangerous compared to acids because alkalis cause liquefactive necrosis
  • • deeper tissue penetration
  • • Option B: Zargar’s grading is used for severity of injury . Zargar’s grading system is a well-established scale used to classify the severity of caustic injuries to the esophagus based on endoscopic findings , helping to guide treatment decisions.
  • • Option B: Zargar’s grading is used for severity of injury
  • • Zargar’s grading system
  • • well-established scale
  • • classify the severity of caustic injuries
  • • esophagus based on endoscopic findings
  • • Option C: It is a premalignant condition . Chronic injury from caustic ingestion can lead to esophageal strictures , which are a known risk factor for the development of esophageal cancer , thus making it a premalignant condition.
  • • Option C: It is a premalignant condition
  • • Chronic injury
  • • caustic ingestion
  • • esophageal strictures
  • • risk factor for the development of esophageal cancer

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ In the context of caustic injuries to the upper gastrointestinal tract , while alkali ingestion is indeed more dangerous, and Zargar’s grading is used to assess the severity of injury , and such injuries can be premalignant , the presence of multiple esophageal ulcers does not universally warrant an urgent cervical esophagostomy.
  • ➤ caustic injuries to the upper gastrointestinal tract
  • ➤ alkali ingestion is indeed more dangerous,
  • ➤ Zargar’s grading is used to assess the severity of injury
  • ➤ injuries can be premalignant
  • ➤ presence of multiple esophageal ulcers does not universally warrant an urgent cervical esophagostomy.
  • ➤ The substance ingested can be a strong acid, causing coagulative necrosis with eschar formation , which may limit penetration to deeper layers, or strong alkali, leading to liquefactive necrosis . The latter potentially penetrates deeper into the oesophageal wall, producing a more severe injury pattern. It is a premalignant condition - Delayed complications include stricture and malignancy. Treatment - Most caustic injuries can be managed conservatively with supportive measures. Deterioration requires surgical treatment, with emergency esophagectomy. The oesophagus can be mobilised transhiatally or via a thoracoscopic approach. Immediate reconstruction is not recommended . A cervical oesophagostomy and a gastrostomy can be done and future reconstruction planned. A feeding jejunostomy is an alternative for nutritional support if the stomach also requires resection.

question: Which of the following is a true statement(s) about capsule endoscopy? It involves swallowing a small camera capsule that takes pictures of the digestive tract. It is used to diagnose conditions such as Crohn's disease and small intestine tumors. The capsule is disposable and does not need to be retrieved after the procedure. It is an invasive procedure that requires sedation.

explanation.detail:
Ans. D) 1,2 and 3

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Capsule endoscopy:
  • • Capsule endoscopy:
  • • It involves swallowing a small camera capsule that takes pictures of the digestive tract - This is a true statement about capsule endoscopy. The camera capsule is about the size of a large vitamin pill and contains a tiny camera and light source. The patient swallows the capsule, which then takes pictures of the digestive tract as it passes through the body. It is used to diagnose conditions such as Crohn's disease and small intestine tumors - This is also a true statement about capsule endoscopy. The procedure is typically used to diagnose conditions that affect the small intestine, such as Crohn's disease, celiac disease, and small intestine tumors. The capsule is disposable and does not need to be retrieved after the procedure - This is another true statement about capsule endoscopy. The camera capsule is disposable and does not need to be retrieved after the procedure. It is passed out of the body in the stool and can be safely disposed of. It is an invasive procedure that requires sedation - This statement is not true. Capsule endoscopy is a non-invasive procedure that does not require sedation . The patient can go about their normal activities during the procedure, although they may be asked to avoid certain foods and medications before and after the procedure.
  • • It involves swallowing a small camera capsule that takes pictures of the digestive tract - This is a true statement about capsule endoscopy. question: Which of the following statements is true regarding hemobilia? It is a rare condition where blood flows into the biliary tree. It is commonly caused by gallstones or inflammation of the gallbladder. Symptoms may include abdominal pain, jaundice, and Malena. Diagnosis is usually made through a liver biopsy.

explanation.detail:
Ans. A. 1 and 3

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • It is a rare condition where blood flows into the biliary tree - This statement is true. Hemobilia is a rare condition where bleeding occurs into the biliary tree . It is commonly caused by gallstones or inflammation of the gallbladder - This statement is not true. While gallstones or inflammation of the gallbladder can cause bleeding into the biliary tree, hemobilia is typically caused by trauma, surgery, or invasive procedures involving the liver or biliary tree. Symptoms of hemobilia may include abdominal pain, jaundice, and Malena (Sandbloom’s triad). Diagnosis is usually made through a liver biopsy - This statement is not entirely true . While a liver biopsy may be used to diagnose hemobilia in some cases, diagnosis is typically made through imaging tests like CECT abdomen .

While gallstones or inflammation of the gallbladder can cause bleeding into the biliary tree, hemobilia is typically caused by trauma, surgery, or invasive procedures involving the liver or biliary tree.
  • • Symptoms of hemobilia may include abdominal pain, jaundice, and Malena (Sandbloom’s triad).
  • • hemobilia
  • • abdominal pain, jaundice, and Malena
  • • (Sandbloom’s triad).
  • • Diagnosis is usually made through a liver biopsy - This statement is not entirely true . While a liver biopsy may be used to diagnose hemobilia in some cases, diagnosis is typically made through imaging tests like CECT abdomen .
  • • not entirely true
  • • diagnosis is typically made through imaging tests like CECT abdomen

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ Hemobilia is a rare condition characterized by bleeding into the biliary tract and is often associated with trauma or iatrogenic injury rather than gallstones or gallbladder inflammation . It typically presents with abdominal pain, jaundice, and melena , and the diagnosis is usually made through imaging and endoscopic procedures , not liver biopsy.

subject: Biochemistry
question: What is the role of Indocyanine green (ICG) dye during laparoscopic cholecystectomy?
options: To evaluate the function of the gallbladder
To identify biliary anatomy and reduce the risk of injury to surrounding structures
To minimize bleeding during surgery by highlighting blood vessels
To reduce the risk of surgical site infection

explanation.short: B. To identify biliary anatomy and reduce the risk of injury to surrounding structures
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) To identify biliary anatomy and reduce the risk of injury to surrounding structures

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option B: To identify biliary anatomy and reduce the risk of injury to surrounding structures . This is the primary use of ICG during laparoscopic cholecystectomy. ICG binds to plasma proteins and is excreted into the bile , providing real-time cholangiography when viewed under near-infrared light , thus enhancing the visualization of the cystic duct, common bile duct, and other biliary structures to prevent injury.
  • • Option B: To identify biliary anatomy and reduce the risk of injury to surrounding structures
  • • This is the primary use
  • • ICG during laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
  • • ICG binds to plasma proteins and is excreted into the bile
  • • real-time cholangiography
  • • under near-infrared light
  • • Option C: To minimize bleeding during surgery by highlighting blood vessels . ICG does help to visualize blood vessels; however, its use in laparoscopic cholecystectomy is more focused on biliary tract visualization rather than to minimize bleeding.
  • • Option C: To minimize bleeding during surgery by highlighting blood vessels
  • • ICG does help to visualize blood vessels;
  • • use in laparoscopic cholecystectomy
  • • focused on biliary tract visualization
  • • Option D: To reduce the risk of surgical site infection . ICG has no antimicrobial properties and does not reduce the risk of surgical site infections .
  • • Option D: To reduce the risk of surgical site infection
  • • ICG has no antimicrobial properties
  • • does not reduce the risk of surgical site infections

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The primary role of Indocyanine green (ICG) dye during laparoscopic cholecystectomy is to enhance the visualization of biliary anatomy , such as the cystic duct and common bile duct , in order to reduce the risk of injury to these and other surrounding structures.
  • ➤ primary role of Indocyanine green
  • ➤ dye
  • ➤ laparoscopic cholecystectomy
  • ➤ enhance the visualization of biliary anatomy
  • ➤ cystic duct
  • ➤ bile duct
  • ➤ Indocyanine green (ICG) dye is used during laparoscopic cholecystectomy to help identify biliary anatomy, such as the common bile duct and cystic duct, and reduce the risk of injury to surrounding structures. ICG dye can also help to highlight blood vessels, which can aid in minimizing bleeding during surgery. However, this is not the main application in cholecystectomy. While ICG dye can be used to evaluate the function of the gallbladder and assess blood flow during surgery, this is not its primary role in laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
  • ➤ Indocyanine green (ICG) dye is used during laparoscopic cholecystectomy to help identify biliary anatomy, such as the common bile duct and cystic duct, and reduce the risk of injury to surrounding structures.
  • ➤ help identify biliary anatomy, such as the common bile duct and cystic duct, and
  • ➤ reduce the risk of injury to surrounding structures.
  • ➤ ICG dye can also help to highlight blood vessels, which can aid in minimizing bleeding during surgery. However, this is not the main application in cholecystectomy.
  • ➤ While ICG dye can be used to evaluate the function of the gallbladder and assess blood flow during surgery, this is not its primary role in laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

subject: Biochemistry
question: Which of these is the ideal donor for organ transplant?
options: Brain dead donor who is an identical twin
Living donor who is an identical twin
Brain dead donor who has the same blood group
Living donor who has the same blood group

explanation.short: B. Living donor who is an identical twin
explanation.detail:
Ans. B) Living donor who is an identical twin

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • Option A. A brain-dead donor who is an identical twin would be an excellent match in terms of HLA compatibility . However, the organs may have suffered from the events leading to brain death and subsequent changes in physiology.
  • • Option A.
  • • excellent match
  • • HLA compatibility
  • • suffered
  • • events leading
  • • brain death
  • • changes in physiology.
  • • Option C . A brain-dead donor who has the same blood group can be a suitable donor , but without the genetic matching of an identical twin , there is a higher risk of rejection .
  • • Option C
  • • can be a suitable donor
  • • without
  • • genetic matching
  • • identical twin
  • • higher risk of rejection
  • • Option D . A living donor who has the same blood group is better than a deceased donor in terms of organ quality, but the risk of rejection would still be higher compared to an identical twin.
  • • Option D
  • • same blood group
  • • better
  • • deceased donor
  • • risk of rejection
  • • still be higher

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The ideal organ donor is a living donor who is an identical twin of the recipient due to the perfect HLA match and the optimal condition of the donated organ , which significantly reduces the risk of rejection and improves transplant outcomes .
  • ➤ identical twin
  • ➤ perfect HLA match
  • ➤ optimal condition
  • ➤ donated organ
  • ➤ reduces
  • ➤ risk of rejection
  • ➤ improves transplant outcomes
  • ➤ Transplants from live donors have a number of advantages over deceased donor organs . Live donor organ from healthy individuals without the comorbidities commonly seen in deceased donors . In the agonal period , before death , deceased donors exhibit marked changes in physiology related to a catecholamine storm and this can cause organ dysfunction . Clearly, live donor organs are not subjected to this insult. Allograft rejection is directed against human leukocyte antigens (HLAs) . These are a group of cell surface glycoprotein molecules. HLA molecules are divided into class I (A, B and C) and class II (DR, DP and DQ). They are highly polymorphic , i.e., their amino acid sequences differ widely between individuals . To give an example, there are > 1000 variants of the HLA-B gene . This genetic variability means that most transplant donors and recipients have different HLA profiles . HLA profiles will be the same in identical twins, hence chance of mismatch is low.
  • ➤ Transplants from live donors have a number of advantages over deceased donor organs . Live donor organ from healthy individuals without the comorbidities commonly seen in deceased donors .

Ans. B) Solubility and buffering capacity will be maximum

  • • buffering
  • • pKa of the histidine side chain
  • • physiological pH.

Educational Objective:

  • ➤ Educational Objective:
  • ➤ The pH at which the molecule carries no net charge is called an isoelectric point or isoelectric pH (pI). At an isoelectric point , the amino acid will carry no net charge, all the groups are ionized but the charges will cancel each other. Hence, at isoelectric point, there is no mobility in an electrical field. Solubility and buffering capacity will be minimum at isoelectric pH. The buffering capacity of plasma proteins and hemoglobin is mainly due to histidine residue.

question: Which one of the following statements is incorrect regarding hemoglobin A (HbA) and hemoglobin S (HbS).
options: Electrophoretically HbS moves faster than HbA at alkaline pH
Structural difference is related to β-chain
HbS is more prone to polymerization under low oxygen conditions
Lack of negative charge of glutamic acid is attributed to the electrophoretic movement

explanation.short: A. Electrophoretically HbS moves faster than HbA at alkaline pH
explanation.detail:
Ans. A. Electrophoretically HbS moves faster than HbA at alkaline pH

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • This statement is false because at alkaline pH (typically ~8.6) during electrophoresis , HbS moves slower than HbA .
  • • false
  • • alkaline pH (typically ~8.6)
  • • electrophoresis
  • • HbS moves slower
  • • HbA
  • • The reason lies in the structural difference :
  • • structural difference
  • • In Hemoglobin A (HbA) , the 6th amino acid of the β-globin chain is glutamic acid , a negatively charged residue at physiological and alkaline pH. In Hemoglobin S (HbS) , the same position is occupied by valine , which is neutral.
  • • In Hemoglobin A (HbA) , the 6th amino acid of the β-globin chain is glutamic acid , a negatively charged residue at physiological and alkaline pH.

  • • glutamic acid

  • • Hemoglobin S (HbS)
  • • valine
  • • This substitution reduces the overall negative charge of HbS compared to HbA, which leads to slower migration toward the anode (positive pole) during electrophoresis at alkaline pH.
  • • negative charge
  • • slower migration toward the anode (positive pole)
  • • Other Options:
  • • Other Options:
  • • Option B) The structural difference is related to the β-chain: Correct — The mutation responsible for HbS is a single point mutation in the β-globin gene . Valine replaces glutamic acid at position 6 of the β-chain. Option C. HbS is more prone to polymerization under low oxygen conditions: Correct — Deoxygenated HbS tends to polymerize, causing red blood cells to assume a sickle shape. Option D) Lack of negative charge of glutamic acid is attributed to the electrophoretic movement: Correct — The substitution eliminates the negative charge of glutamic acid, reducing the net negative charge of HbS, which slows down its migration on electrophoresis at alkaline pH.

question: A 3.5-year-old girl of Arabian descent presents to the clinic with severe anemia, thrombocytopenia, leukocytosis, and elevated ESR. options: RFLP result from a single base change
Single nucleotide change results in change of Glutamate to Valine
HbS confers resistance against malaria in heterozygotes
‘Sticky patch’ is generated as a result of replacement of a nonpolar residue with a polar residue in Sickle Cell Anemia (HbS)

explanation.short: D. ‘Sticky patch’ is generated as a result of replacement of a nonpolar residue with a polar residue in Sickle Cell Anemia (HbS)
explanation.detail:
Ans. D) ‘Sticky patch’ is generated as a result of replacement of a nonpolar residue with a polar residue in Sickle Cell Anemia (HbS) — this is False.

Explanation:

  • • Explanation:
  • • In sickle cell anemia , the amino acid substitution involves replacing a polar residue Glutamate with a nonpolar residue (valine) at the 6th position of the β-globin chain.
  • • sickle cell anemia
  • • polar residue Glutamate
  • • nonpolar residue (valine)
  • • This substitution creates a hydrophobic “sticky patch” on the surface of the deoxygenated hemoglobin S (HbS) molecule. These hydrophobic patches interact abnormally with each other, leading to polymerization of HbS inside red cells, which causes the characteristic "sickling" shape of RBCs under hypoxic conditions.
  • • hydrophobic “sticky patch”
  • • polymerization of HbS
  • • So the false statement is that the “sticky patch” arises from a nonpolar → polar substitution — the reality is the opposite: → It’s a polar (glutamate) → nonpolar (valine) substitution.
  • • nonpolar → polar substitution
  • • polar (glutamate) → nonpolar (valine)
  • • Other Options:
  • • Other Options:
  • • Option A) RFLP result from a single base change: Correct — The sickle cell mutation involves a single nucleotide change ( A → T at the 6th codon), which eliminates a restriction enzyme site. This difference can be detected using Restriction Fragment Length Polymorphism (RFLP) analysis. Option B) Single nucleotide change results in change of Glutamate to Valine: Correction: this statement is Correct . Option C) HbS confers resistance against malaria in heterozygotes: Correct — Heterozygotes for the HbS gene (sickle cell trait) are relatively protected against Plasmodium falciparum malaria , explaining its evolutionary persistence in malaria-endemic regions.
  • • Option A) RFLP result from a single base change: Correct — The sickle cell mutation involves a single nucleotide change ( A → T at the 6th codon), which eliminates a restriction enzyme site. This difference can be detected using Restriction Fragment Length Polymorphism (RFLP) analysis.
  • • Option A) RFLP result from a single base change:
  • • A → T
  • • Restriction Fragment Length Polymorphism (RFLP)
  • • Option B) Single nucleotide change results in change of Glutamate to Valine: Correction: this statement is Correct .

  • • Correct

  • • Plasmodium falciparum malaria

question: Assertion (A) : ALA synthase is the rate-limiting enzyme of HEME synthesis, regulated allosterically. Reasoning (R): If there is excess of free heme, Fe++ is oxidized to Fe+++ forming hematin, which in turn regulates the enzyme. Assess the statements.
